content:
- Ran every required quality lane cleanly: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, and `make test-long` all passed. The long lane completed successfully, including the ignored container-image test and the ignored multi-database migration suite.
- Updated the task markdown to `<status>completed</status>` and `<passes>true</passes>`, checked all acceptance boxes, and ran `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, which refreshed `current_tasks.md` / `current_tasks_done.md` and sent the completion email.
- Next: review the final repo status, commit all task-related files (including `.ralph` state/progress files) with evidence in the message, push, and then stop.

- Read prior progress and current/done Ralph task summaries.
- Confirmed the active backlog currently shows two failing Story 14 report tasks and the last bug is already marked passes=true in done tasks.
- Next I am checking the actual task files for blockers/priority and reading a small codebase slice to ground the choice in the real stack.

- Inspected the two active Story 14 task files directly.
- Neither active task declares blockers or priority tags; both are unblocked failing tasks in the same story.
- Observed the actual codebase is a Rust workspace centered on crates/runner with supporting crates/source-bootstrap and crates/ingest-contract; refreshing Ralph summary and reading top-level docs/manifests now before selecting.

- Refreshed Ralph task summaries and confirmed the active backlog is still exactly the two Story 14 failing tasks.
- Read README.md and Cargo.toml to ground the repo identity: a Rust 2024 workspace with runner, source-bootstrap, and ingest-contract crates, centered on operator CLI/runtime workflows.
- Selection decision: choose Story 14 task 01 first because both tasks are unblocked and same-priority, and the novice README/manual-flow report is the more user-facing, sequence-first validation before an internal code-complexity audit.

- Chose the next task and wrote it to .ralph/current_task.txt.
- Selected .ralph/tasks/story-14-reports/01-task-report-novice-user-manual-experience.md because there are no remaining unblocked failing bugs, both active Story 14 tasks are unblocked failing non-bugs with no priority tags, and the novice README/manual-flow report is the more user-facing first validation of the actual Rust workspace and public operator contract.
- Next agent should execute that Story 14 novice-user report task, produce the required artifacts, and keep make check / make test / make lint green.

- Read Ralph progress, current task pointer, the Story 14 novice-user report task file, README.md, and the required `tdd` / `improve-code-boundaries` skill material before changing anything.
- Determined this task is still in planning mode because the task file is `not_started`, so this turn should create a concrete execution plan rather than begin the novice-user trial.
- Added the plan file at `.ralph/tasks/story-14-reports/01-task-report-novice-user-manual-experience_plans/2026-04-19-novice-user-report-plan.md` with a README-first execution strategy, vertical-slice TDD checkpoints, required artifact paths, evidence-capture rules, quality gates, and a boundary rule to avoid duplicating operator-contract knowledge if rescue fixes are needed.
- Next agent should review the new plan, flip the final marker from `TO BE VERIFIED` to `NOW EXECUTE` once satisfied, then quit immediately per task instructions. A later execution turn should follow the plan to perform the actual novice-user trial, produce the report artifacts, and run `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, and `make test-long` before closing the task.

- Re-read the generated plan and judged it concrete enough to execute as written.
- Flipped the plan terminator from `TO BE VERIFIED` to `NOW EXECUTE` in `.ralph/tasks/story-14-reports/01-task-report-novice-user-manual-experience_plans/2026-04-19-novice-user-report-plan.md`.
- Per the task instructions, this turn stops here without beginning the manual novice-user trial.

- Read Ralph progress, current task pointer, and the required `tdd` / `improve-code-boundaries` skill material.
- Confirmed the active task is `.ralph/tasks/story-14-reports/01-task-report-novice-user-manual-experience.md`; now reading the task file and its existing plan to determine whether this turn must execute or stop after design review.
- Next: inspect the task status and plan terminator, then either perform the manual novice-user execution path or quit immediately if the instructions still require a planning-only stop.

- Read the active task file and the existing novice-user report plan.
- Confirmed the plan terminator is `NOW EXECUTE`, so this turn must perform the README-first manual trial and produce the required report artifacts.
- Next: inspect README and repo-level docs, then execute the documented operator flow in vertical slices while recording exact commands, confusions, blockers, and README sufficiency.

- Read `README.md` as the primary public operator contract and noted the major surfaces under test: source-bootstrap, Docker quick start, schema/setup workflow, runtime startup, and cutover docs.
- Started the actual novice-user execution by checking prerequisite binaries (`cargo`, `docker`, `openssl`, `cockroach`, `pg_dump`, `molt`) instead of assuming the environment is ready, because README currently does not present an explicit prerequisite section.
- Next: create a clean trial workspace, attempt the documented source-bootstrap and Docker flows exactly as written, and record every blocker, ambiguity, and extra lookup.

- Created the required report artifact files and a contained trial workspace under `.ralph/tasks/story-14-reports/artifacts/report-novice-user/workdir/` so the manual run can be reproduced without scattering scratch files through the repo root.
- Began Slice 2 by running the documented `cargo run -p source-bootstrap -- render-bootstrap-script ...` command against the README example config.
- Next: inspect the rendered bootstrap script, attempt the documented manual execution step, and capture exactly where the novice path stops being self-sufficient.

- The first README source-bootstrap command failed immediately: `source-bootstrap` refuses to read the config because `webhook.ca_cert_path` points at `ca.crt`, but the source quick start never explains how a novice should create or obtain that certificate.
- Treating this as the first clear README insufficiency; now checking CLI help before applying the smallest recovery step needed to continue the manual trial.
- Next: generate a local CA certificate only as a recovery action, rerun the render step, and keep that gap explicit in the report.

- `source-bootstrap --help` added no recovery guidance beyond `--config`, so the README failure remains unresolved from the public interface itself.
- Proceeding with the smallest possible recovery step: generate a local CA certificate file where the README config expects one, then rerun the documented bootstrap render.
- Next: inspect the rendered script and attempt `bash cockroach-bootstrap.sh` to see whether the novice path reaches a real source-side execution step or just another hidden prerequisite.

- Generated a CA certificate and reran the documented source-bootstrap render command; it now exits successfully.
- First inspection shows an unexpected zero-byte redirected output file, so I am verifying whether the command writes nothing, writes elsewhere, or has another UX trap around stdout/output expectations.
- Next: inspect the rendered file directly and rerun without redirection to confirm what the CLI actually emits before attempting the manual script execution step.

- Slice 2 confirmed two separate README gaps: the source-bootstrap example config references a missing CA certificate, and the rendered script then fails immediately because the `cockroach` CLI is not installed or documented as a prerequisite.
- Moving to Slice 3 now by following the Docker quick start: generate the TLS files exactly as documented and build the destination image from the repository root.
- Next: run `validate-config` through the image entrypoint and measure whether the destination path is smoother or surfaces a second wave of hidden prerequisites.

- Slice 3 destination quick start is materially smoother so far: TLS generation and `docker run ... validate-config` both worked as written once the cert files existed.
- Starting Slice 4 now by attempting the documented CockroachDB schema export and PostgreSQL schema dump commands exactly as shown, using the example hostnames and mapping values.
- Next: record whether schema export failures come from undocumented prerequisites, unreachable example infrastructure, or confusing workflow expectations, then continue with the manual setup/render steps that do not require live database access.

- Confirmed `render-postgres-setup` succeeds and emits artifacts even without live database access, which is the strongest novice-friendly step after `validate-config`.
- Continuing Slice 4 by inspecting the generated setup README and probing `compare-schema` / `render-helper-plan` with the schema files left behind by the failed export steps, to see whether later-stage errors help the user recover or just compound confusion.
- Next: inspect those command errors, then attempt `runner run --config` to measure runtime-startup clarity when the destination host is still just the README example.

- Inspected later schema/setup behavior: failed export commands leave zero-byte files, and `compare-schema` / `render-helper-plan` then report missing tables rather than pointing the novice back to the failed export prerequisites.
- Moving into Slice 5 now by checking the generated per-mapping PostgreSQL setup instructions and attempting `runner run --config /config/runner.yml` against the README example host values.
- Next: capture the runtime startup failure mode, then synthesize the README sufficiency verdict and prioritized simplification recommendations into the required artifact files.

- Runtime startup without host-port publishing reached a real runner error: it immediately fails to connect to the example PostgreSQL host with `Name does not resolve`, which at least clearly exposes the next missing prerequisite.
- As one explicit post-README lookup, I am now reading `investigations/cockroach-webhook-cdc/README.md` because live containers with that name are already occupying the documented ports, and the top-level README claimed a novice should not need to inspect `investigations/`.
- Next: fold that extra-investigation result into the report, then write the four required artifacts and run the full quality gates.

- Wrote the four required report artifacts with the actual manual-trial evidence, a README sufficiency verdict, a full command trace, a severity-ordered friction log, and prioritized simplification recommendations.
- Beginning the required quality gates now: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, and `make test-long` must all pass before the task file can be marked as passing.
- Next: finish the four gates, update the task metadata/checklists, run `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, commit, push, and stop.

- All required quality gates passed: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, and `make test-long` all completed successfully.
- Updated the novice-user report task file to completed/passes=true and checked every acceptance box.
- Next: run `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, review the final diff/status, commit all task-related files with evidence in the message, push, and stop.
